learning_outcomes.description: Analyse human needs, values, behaviours, and experiences within complex socio-technical contexts using appropriate contextual inquiry, qualitative, and quantitative research methods.

Synthesise HCI theories, socio-technical perspectives, and research evidence to explain how technologies, people, organisations, infrastructures, policies, cultures, and wider systems shape each other.

Develop and justify socio-technical systems maps that identify actors, boundaries, relationships, feedback loops, tensions, leverage points, and broader conditions shaping human experience and technological practice

Design, prototype, and evaluate technology-based interventions that respond to socio-technical problems, considering usability, accessibility, inclusion, implementation constraints, stakeholder needs, and human experience.

Critically evaluate technological interventions across multiple system layers, including stakeholder relationships, organisational workflows, technical and data infrastructures, ethical concerns, governance, equity, cultural safety, sustainability, and unintended consequences.

Justify the strategic selection and responsible integration of diverse knowledge sources, including formal research, practitioner knowledge, crowdsourced documentation, and generative systems, within complex problem-solving workflows, demonstrating intellectual accountability for the synthesised outcome
Establish, enact, evaluate and adapt accountable collaborative practices to support effective teamwork, shared decision-making and the achievement of collective outcomes.

assessments.task_description:
In this individual assessment, you will select an approved Sustainable Development Goal target and define a manageable problem situation within it. You will investigate the wider socio-technical system and create an evidence-informed map showing the consequential people, organisations, technologies, practices, rules, resources, relationships and system dynamics that contribute to the problem.

Through this assessment, you will learn how to frame a complex problem, define and justify a system boundary, identify dependencies and power relationships, and use diverse knowledge sources to build a defensible interpretation of a socio-technical system. You will also identify uncertainties and directions for later human-centred inquiry.

This assessment item ensures that students are provided with feedback prior to Census week (excluding situations where students have extensions approved beyond the census date).

Information Sources (including AI Tools)

You may use Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools (including Generative AI and Machine Translation tools), and other external sources or systems to support this assessment. Their use is optional, and choosing not to use AI tools will not disadvantage you.

You remain responsible for determining what knowledge and assistance are appropriate, evaluating the relevance and limitations of contributions, verifying consequential claims, and deciding what should influence your final system map and analysis. Any use of AI tools must be acknowledged in accordance with the course requirements. Your Accountable Knowledge Integration Statement must explain the role of any consequential human or technological contributions and how you evaluated and integrated them. Failure to acknowledge AI tools use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The A1 outputs will be submitted to Learn.UQ. Further information about submission will be available in the assessment brief (also available in Learn.UQ).
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
In this group assessment, your team will bring together relevant knowledge from A1, define a focused opportunity for intervention, develop a prototype and conduct a mixed-method evaluation of the interface and immediate human–system interaction. You will collect and analyse qualitative and quantitative evidence, refine the prototype where appropriate, and document the inquiry through a collaborative research notebook.

Through this assessment, you will learn how to plan and conduct an accountable collaborative inquiry, develop a prototype for evaluation, apply qualitative and quantitative methods, and integrate different forms of evidence into defensible findings. You will also develop teamwork capabilities and learn to distinguish between what an evaluation demonstrates, what it suggests and what remains uncertain.

Information Sources (including AI Tools)

Your team may use Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools (including Generative AI and Machine Translation tools), and other external sources or systems to support this assessment. Their use is optional, and choosing not to use AI tools will not disadvantage the team.

The team remains collectively accountable for deciding what forms of assistance are appropriate, setting boundaries on their use, evaluating and verifying consequential contributions, and integrating them into a coherent inquiry. Individual team members must also understand and be able to explain contributions that materially affect the shared prototype, evaluation, analysis or findings. Any use of AI tools must be acknowledged in accordance with the course requirements and made visible where it materially shaped the work. Failure to acknowledge AI tool use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The A2 outputs will be submitted to Learn.UQ as a PDF. Further information about submission will be available in the assessment brief (also available in Learn.UQ).
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
A3.1 Layered Systemic Evaluation Report - submission is due at 2pm on 9 Nov 2026. 9/11/2026 - 9/11/2026

A3.2 Critical Discussion - Times will be made available for you to book a specific time to during the period 10 Nov to 13 Nov, 2026 10/11/2026 - 13/11/2026
assessments.task_description:
In this individual assessment, you will extend the A2 project beyond the immediate interaction and examine what may happen if the proposed system enters a wider real-world context. A3 has two components.

  • In A3.1, you will individually prepare a Layered Systemic Evaluation Report that extends the A2 project beyond the immediate interaction and examines what may happen if the proposed system enters a wider real-world context. You will define an evaluation focus, select relevant systemic layers, construct an appropriate evidence base, analyse dependencies, risks, benefits, burdens, values and power relationships, and develop a reasoned recommendation about what should happen next.
  • In A3.2, you will participate in an individual critical discussion with a member of the teaching team. This discussion will require you to explain and defend the focus, method, evidence, analysis and recommendations presented in your report, and to respond to questions that test your independent understanding. In accordance with UQ Assessment Procedures, this discussion will be video recorded.

Across both components, you will learn how to design and conduct a bounded systemic evaluation, integrate project, contextual, comparative and anticipatory evidence, and make proportionate recommendations under conditions of uncertainty. You will develop greater independence in selecting methods, evaluating knowledge and defending your final judgement.

Information sources (including AI Tools)

A3.1: Layered Systemic Evaluation Report

You may use Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools (including Generative AI and Machine Translation tools), and other external sources or systems to support the preparation of your report. Their use is optional, and choosing not to use AI tools will not disadvantage you.

You are independently responsible for selecting the knowledge and assistance required, evaluating the credibility, relevance and limitations of contributions, determining what verification is proportionate, and defending the resulting analysis and recommendations. Any use of AI tools must be acknowledged in accordance with the course requirements and made visible where it materially influenced your evaluation method, evidence, analysis or judgement. You must be able to explain and defend all parts of the submitted report independently. Failure to acknowledge AI tool use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

A3.2: Critical Discussion

This assessment task is to be completed in person. The use of AI tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI tools may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 1 To Week 13(27 Jul - 01 Nov)
learning_activities.topic:
Weekly Lecture

Weekly lectures introduce and explain the key concepts, theories, methods and examples that underpin the course. They will develop your understanding of socio-technical systems, human experience, evaluation, systems thinking, ethical and cultural responsibility, and accountable knowledge integration. Lectures provide the conceptual foundation needed to interpret the assessment tasks and make informed decisions throughout the course.
learning_activities.topic_description: Weekly Lecture Weekly lectures introduce and explain the key concepts, theories, methods and examples that underpin the course. They will develop your understanding of socio-technical systems, human experience, evaluation, systems thinking, ethical and cultural responsibility, and accountable knowledge integration. Lectures provide the conceptual foundation needed to interpret the assessment tasks and make informed decisions throughout the course.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 1 To Week 13(27 Jul - 01 Nov)
learning_activities.topic:
Weekly Applied Class

Each week, you will participate in one three-hour applied class. These classes are designed to directly support the completion of the assessment tasks by helping you apply course concepts and methods to your own project work.

Applied classes will include structured activities, practical exercises, collaborative work, tool training, project development and formative feedback. They may also provide additional or more specific guidance about how assessment requirements should be interpreted and completed. Activities will progressively support system mapping, teamwork, prototyping, evaluation design, qualitative and quantitative analysis, layered systemic evaluation and preparation for the individual critical discussion. Because the assessment tasks build across the semester, regular participation in applied classes is important for keeping pace with the project and developing the capabilities assessed in the course.
